content.text: Why Skills Gap Analysis Comes Before Training Design

A training programme designed without a skills gap analysis is a guess. It targets the competencies the programme team thinks are missing based on what they have seen in similar organisations, not what is actually missing in this organisation, in these roles, at this moment. The result is almost always misallocated investment: intensive training on competencies where the gap is already small, and underinvestment on the competencies where the gap is genuinely blocking adoption.
A skills gap analysis has one purpose: tell you
where to spend the training budget first
. It produces a prioritised list of role-competency gaps ordered by size and strategic importance, which directly drives the curriculum design and training delivery sequence.

This document covers the eight GenAI competency domains, the four-level proficiency scale, how to assess current levels without creating an assessment burden, and the Python model for computing and prioritising gaps.
The Eight GenAI Competency Domains
GenAI competence in a business context spans eight domains. Not all domains are relevant to all roles — the gap analysis weights competencies by role relevance before computing gap size.
Domain
Definition
Primarily Relevant To
Prompt Engineering
Ability to write clear, constrained prompts that reliably produce useful outputs; iterative refinement
All roles — foundational
AI Output Evaluation
Ability to identify errors, hallucinations, and bias in AI outputs; verify against sources
All roles — foundational
Workflow Integration
Ability to embed AI tools into existing task flows without creating bottlenecks or duplication
Business analysts, team leads, process owners
AI Risk and Compliance
Understanding of data classification, acceptable use policies, disclosure requirements, and liability
All roles — foundational; critical for legal, risk, HR
Data Literacy
Ability to interpret AI outputs that reference data; understand confidence and uncertainty
Analysts, developers, data-adjacent roles
Model Selection
Ability to choose appropriate models or tools for specific task types; understand capability tradeoffs
Developers, architects, product managers
Ethics and Bias Awareness
Ability to recognise when AI output reflects or amplifies bias; apply fairness considerations
HR, hiring managers, customer-facing roles, team leads
Change Leadership
Ability to lead a team through AI adoption: communicate change, manage resistance, model new behaviours
Team leads, managers, HR, L&D

attrs.alt: GenAI Skills Gap Heat Map — Role x Competency

content.text: The heat map shows illustrative gap sizes for six role archetypes across the eight domains. Developers show small gaps in most technical domains but a large gap in Change Leadership — they can use AI but may not be able to help their team adopt it. HR/L&D shows large gaps in technical domains and small gaps in ethics — the inverse pattern.
The Four-Level Proficiency Scale
Use a four-level scale consistently across all competency domains so gaps are comparable:
Level
Label
Behavioural Descriptor
Unaware
Does not know the competency exists or is relevant to their role
Aware
Understands the concept and why it matters; cannot yet apply it independently
Practitioner
Applies the competency independently on standard tasks; seeks help on novel situations
Expert
Applies the competency on complex, novel tasks; coaches others; contributes to policy

The gap is always (target level) - (current level). A Knowledge Worker who is at Level 1 on Prompt Engineering but needs to be at Level 2 has a gap of 1 — a MEDIUM gap. The same person at Level 0 needing Level 3 has a gap of 3 — always a LARGE gap requiring significant investment.
Assessing Current Proficiency
Formal skills assessments create resentment and low response rates when done badly. Use three lightweight instruments in combination:
Self-Assessment Survey (5 minutes per employee)

Ask each employee to rate their current proficiency on each relevant domain using the four-level scale. Self-assessments are biased upward (people rate themselves higher than they perform), but the bias is consistent, which means relative gaps between roles and domains are still informative even if absolute levels are overstated.
Manager Corroboration (Sample-Based)
Do not ask managers to rate all their direct reports — the survey burden will kill response rates. Instead, ask managers to rate the
3-5 employees they know best

on the two or three domains most relevant to the team's work. Use this data to calibrate the self-assessment for that team (compute the average self-vs-manager delta and apply it as the calibration factor for uncorroborated employees).
Observed Performance Signal
The champion interaction log and artefact peer-review data provide indirect skill signals without any additional survey burden:
Prompt quality score from peer review (1-5 rubric): employees who have submitted prompts have an observed Prompt Engineering signal.
Office hours questions: champions can tag questions by competency domain. A high volume of 'how do I know if this is right?' questions from a team maps to an Output Evaluation gap.
Artefact reuse: employees who regularly cite and adapt others' prompts are demonstrating Workflow Integration competence.
Aggregating to Role-Level Gap Maps
The
priority_interventions()

output drives the curriculum design directly: LARGE gaps with many affected employees become the first training cohort; SMALL gaps are addressed through self-serve artefacts rather than structured training.
Translating Gap Map to Training Investment
Use a simple investment matrix to allocate training resources:
Gap Size
Employees Affected
Recommended Investment
Delivery Format
LARGE
Many (> 30% of role)
Structured cohort training with champion follow-up
Workshop + buddy + 30-day practice sprint
LARGE
Few (< 15% of role)
Targeted individual support
Champion-led 1:1 sessions + curated artefact path
MEDIUM
Many
Self-serve with structured prompt and use case library
Artefact library + prompt-of-the-week + office hours
MEDIUM
Few
Self-serve materials with champion on-demand
Artefact library + on-demand champion access
SMALL
Any
Maintenance: incorporate into onboarding and refreshers
Onboarding materials + quarterly newsletter
Updating the Gap Analysis
A skills gap analysis conducted once and never repeated is a historical artefact, not a management tool. Run a lightweight refresh quarterly:
Update self-assessments for employees who completed a training intervention — measure whether assessed levels improved.
Pull new observed signals from the champion interaction log and artefact peer reviews.
Recompute role-level gap maps and compare to the prior quarter — identify whether interventions are closing gaps at the expected rate.
Adjust training investment: close out SMALL gaps from the structured training backlog and bring in the next LARGE gap from the priority list.
Setting Role-Specific Target Proficiency Levels

Target levels should not be set by the programme team alone. A programme-imposed target that is too high creates unnecessary anxiety; a target that is too low creates a false sense of readiness. Use a three-stakeholder process:

Business owner input: ask the BU head what the role needs to do with GenAI in the next 12 months. A team lead who needs to run team-level AI strategy sessions needs Change Leadership Level 3; one who simply needs to support their team's tool use needs Level 2.

Role incumbent input: run a 30-minute structured conversation with 2-3 incumbents from each role asking: 'What GenAI-related tasks do you think your role will require in 12 months that you can't do well today?' Map their answers to the eight competency domains.

HR/L&D validation: compare the resulting targets against what is achievable within the available training budget and timeline. Adjust downward where targets are aspirational rather than operationally necessary.
The
ld_feasibility_ceiling

is set by the L&D team based on available programme hours, champion capacity, and the typical learning curve for moving from one level to the next. Moving from Level 0 to Level 2 in a 12-month period is feasible for most knowledge workers; moving from Level 0 to Level 3 is not, and setting that as a target creates a false failure.
Competency Domain Priorities by Organisational Context
The eight domains are not equally important in all organisational contexts. Weight them by the organisation's risk profile and strategic use of GenAI:
Organisational Context
Highest-Priority Domains
Rationale
Client-facing professional services
AI Risk and Compliance, Ethics and Bias Awareness, Output Evaluation
Client deliverables have high accountability; errors or bias have reputational and legal consequences
Software product organisation
Prompt Engineering, Model Selection, Workflow Integration
Developer productivity is the primary GenAI value driver; technical depth matters more than risk framing
Large enterprise with diverse workforce
Change Leadership, Prompt Engineering, Output Evaluation
Cultural adoption at scale requires leaders who can model and support the change, not just individuals who can use the tool
Financial services / regulated industry
AI Risk and Compliance, Output Evaluation, Ethics and Bias Awareness
Regulatory scrutiny of AI use requires foundational compliance knowledge across all roles
HR and talent function
Ethics and Bias Awareness, Change Leadership, AI Risk and Compliance
Hiring, performance, and development decisions made with AI assistance carry bias risk and regulatory exposure

Prioritising domains by organisational context narrows the skills gap analysis to the competencies that matter most for your specific situation. Running a full eight-domain analysis for every role is comprehensive but dilutes the intervention focus — tackle the top three domains for your context before expanding.
Common Skills Gap Analysis Pitfalls
Pitfall 1: Treating Self-Assessment as Ground Truth

Unverified self-assessments inflate the picture. A team that self-reports as Level 2 Prompt Engineering but consistently writes one-sentence prompts with no constraints is at Level 1 or below. Always apply a calibration factor to self-report data and corroborate at least a sample with manager observation or observed performance signals before using the gap map to set curriculum priorities.
Pitfall 2: Running the Analysis Once

A skills gap analysis conducted at programme launch and never updated will show LARGE gaps shrinking to MEDIUM on paper (because training was delivered) while actual performance remains flat (because the training did not transfer to behaviour). Quarterly refreshes using the observed performance signals from champion logs and artefact reviews reveal whether the gap is actually closing or just being credited on the tracking spreadsheet.
Pitfall 3: Using Gap Size Alone to Set Priorities

A LARGE gap in Model Selection for Knowledge Workers is genuinely large — but Model Selection is not in the target profile for Knowledge Workers, so the gap is irrelevant. Always filter gap size by role relevance. Only gaps where the target proficiency level is above zero are actionable training investments; gaps in domains outside the role's required competency set are not gaps — they are appropriate specialisation.
Pitfall 4: Forgetting That Target Levels Must Rise Over Time

The GenAI landscape changes fast enough that a Level 2 Prompt Engineering practitioner today needs to be at Level 3 in 18 months, as the tools evolve and the organisation's ambition grows. Build a target escalation schedule into the gap analysis: when 80% of a role cohort reaches a target level, raise the target by one level for the next cycle. The gap analysis should never show zero gaps across the board — that would mean the organisation has stopped learning.
Summary

A GenAI skills gap analysis maps current employee proficiency across eight competency domains against role-specific target levels on a four-level scale. The gap (target minus calibrated current level) is computed per employee and aggregated to role-level averages that drive the training investment matrix.

Assess current proficiency through self-assessment surveys (calibrated for inflation), manager corroboration on a sample basis, and observed performance signals from champion logs and artefact reviews. Prioritise interventions by gap size and number of employees affected: LARGE gaps affecting many employees get structured cohort training; SMALL gaps get self-serve materials. Refresh the gap map quarterly so the curriculum stays aligned with actual skill levels rather than what they were at launch.
